scenarioName: Upper GI Bleed — Hematemesis and Melena with Hemorrhagic Shock

scenarioStory.history: 68-year-old male with a history of alcohol use, cirrhosis, hypertension, and recent hospitalization for atrial fibrillation (now on apixaban) developed dyspepsia and dark stools over the past two days but didn't seek care. Tonight he had a sudden episode of vomiting bright-red blood with clots — initially he thought it was about a cup, then a second large episode 20 minutes later. His wife called 911 when he became pale, sweaty, and almost passed out trying to walk to the bathroom. He has been having melena (black tarry stools) for two days. No abdominal pain other than mild epigastric burning. No reported chest pain, headache, or trauma. He drinks 6-8 beers daily and stopped 'a few days ago' before his AFib admission. Last apixaban dose was this morning.
scenarioStory.course: Initial Contact: EMS finds a 68-year-old anticoagulated cirrhotic patient in hemorrhagic shock from a likely variceal bleed. He has had at least 2 episodes of large-volume hematemesis, ongoing melena for 2 days, and is now hypotensive, tachycardic, with cool clammy skin and altered mental status. This is the initial contact phase: dispatch, SAMPLE history, scene findings, and primary survey drive the response. → Transient response: After 1 L NS, patient is briefly more responsive, BP up to 96 systolic, HR down to 112. This is a transient response — classic Class III shock physiology. Crew continues rapid transport with serial reassessment, recognizing that crystalloid is a bridge, not a definitive fix in active hemorrhage.

patientInformation.patientCondition: Multiple episodes of bright-red vomiting and black tarry stools, now weak and lightheaded

scenarioEvents.name: Initial Contact
scenarioEvents.description: EMS finds a 68-year-old anticoagulated cirrhotic patient in hemorrhagic shock from a likely variceal bleed. He has had at least 2 episodes of large-volume hematemesis, ongoing melena for 2 days, and is now hypotensive, tachycardic, with cool clammy skin and altered mental status. This is the initial contact phase: dispatch, SAMPLE history, scene findings, and primary survey drive the response.
relatedChecklist: Recognize hemorrhagic shock (HR 132, BP 78/48, EtCO2 26, AMS, pallor)
High-flow O2 via NRB; titrate to 94-98%
Two large-bore IVs (16-18g); IO if no IV in 2 attempts
NS 500 mL bolus pressure-infused; reassess; up to 30 mL/kg (≈2.3 L)
Request blood products if available; pre-arrival notification
Cardiac monitor + 12-lead (afib known; rule out demand ischemia)
Place waveform EtCO2 (26 mmHg = poor perfusion)
Document anticoagulant (apixaban — last dose timing) for ED
Rapid transport with pre-arrival notification (massive GI bleed, on apixaban)

scenarioEvents.name: Transient response
scenarioEvents.description: After 1 L NS, patient is briefly more responsive, BP up to 96 systolic, HR down to 112. This is a transient response — classic Class III shock physiology. Crew continues rapid transport with serial reassessment, recognizing that crystalloid is a bridge, not a definitive fix in active hemorrhage.
relatedChecklist: Ondansetron 4 mg IV/SL (after 12-lead — afib + meds = QT risk)
Reassess vitals q3-5min; expect transient response only
Watch for rales/JVD (cirrhotic patient — third-spacing risk)
Keep yankauer + battery suction ready and pre-tested

scenarioEvents.name: Rebleed with aspiration
scenarioEvents.description: During transport, patient has a third large hematemesis. Because the crew did not position laterally or have suction immediately available, aspiration occurs. SpO2 drops to the high 80s, the airway is contaminated with blood, and the crew now needs aggressive SALAD-style suction, BVM with PEEP, and possibly an advanced airway. This branch teaches the cost of skipping anticipatory airway preparation in massive GI bleed.
relatedChecklist: Place in lateral recovery position; head up if BP allows
Two-person BVM with PEEP 5; tidal volume just to chest rise
Consider SGA or intubation if BVM fails to oxygenate
Emergent transport — ED prep for massive transfusion
